cwe: CWE-350 - Reliance on Reverse DNS Resolution for a Security-Critical Action
CWE-1321 - Improperly Controlled Modification of Object Prototype Attributes
CWE-923 - Improper Restriction of Communication Channel to Intended Endpoints

threat.title: Subdomain Takeover via Dangling DNS Records
threat.description: Subdomain takeover occurs when a DNS record (typically CNAME) points to an external service that has been deprovisioned or is no longer claimed, allowing an attacker to register the same resource and serve malicious content on the legitimate subdomain. This creates a trust exploitation vector where users believe they are interacting with the legitimate organization.

attack.scenario:
1. Reconnaissance: Attacker enumerates subdomains using tools like Subfinder, Amass, or certificate transparency logs (crt.sh)
2. Dangling DNS Identification: Scans for CNAME records pointing to unclaimed cloud resources (e.g., example.s3.amazonaws.com, username.github.io)
3. Service Detection: Identifies the cloud service provider and checks if the resource is available using tools like can-i-take-over-xyz, Subjack, or Nuclei
4. Resource Claiming: Attacker registers the unclaimed resource (e.g., creates S3 bucket with same name, claims GitHub Pages site)
5. Malicious Content Deployment: Serves phishing pages, malware, or credential harvesters that appear to come from the trusted domain
6. Exploitation: Users trust the subdomain due to valid SSL certificate and legitimate domain name, leading to data theft or system compromise
prerequisites: Target organization has deprovisioned cloud resources without removing DNS records
DNS CNAME record points to external cloud service (S3, GitHub Pages, Azure, etc.)
Cloud resource name is available for registration by third parties
No CAA DNS records or domain verification requirements in place

cve_references.description: Subdomain takeover is a misconfiguration vulnerability, not typically assigned CVE numbers, but documented in OWASP testing guides

real_world_breaches.impact: Multiple Starbucks subdomains (svcgatewayus.starbucks.com, mydailydev.starbucks.com) exposed to takeover via abandoned Azure resources; $4,000 in bug bounties paid
real_world_breaches.root_cause: DNS records pointed to deprovisioned Microsoft Azure resources that were never cleaned up

real_world_breaches.incident: Uber Authentication Bypass via Subdomain Takeover
real_world_breaches.impact: Authentication bypass on auth.uber.com through subdomain takeover of saostatic.uber.com; valid session cookies stolen via SSO due to cookie sharing
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Dangling CNAME record pointing to unclaimed S3 bucket allowed attacker to serve malicious content on Uber subdomain

real_world_breaches.impact: Donald Trump fundraising website defaced through subdomain takeover attack, causing reputational damage and potential donor confusion
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Abandoned subdomain with dangling DNS record pointed to unclaimed third-party service

bug_bounty_reports.summary: Starbucks mydailydev.starbucks.com subdomain takeover - 121 upvotes on HackerOne disclosure
HackerOne's own info.hacker.one subdomain takeover via GitHub Pages - 133 upvotes, demonstrating even security-focused companies are vulnerable
Snapchat's fastly.sc-cdn.net subdomain takeover via Fastly CDN - $3,000 bounty with 114 upvotes, one of the highest-paid subdomain takeover reports

security_research.title: A Guide To Subdomain Takeovers 2.0
security_research.key_finding: 167 publicly disclosed subdomain takeovers compiled from HackerOne's Hacktivity, with GitHub Pages, AWS S3, and Azure being the most commonly exploited services

security_research.title: Can I Take Over XYZ - Subdomain Takeover Reference
security_research.key_finding: Community-maintained list of 76+ services vulnerable to subdomain takeover, serving as the definitive reference for security researchers

security_research.title: Microsoft Azure Subdomain Takeover Prevention Guide
security_research.key_finding: Microsoft Defender for App Service includes automated dangling DNS detection; PowerShell tool 'Get-DanglingDnsRecords' helps identify vulnerable DNS entries

mitigations.title: Implement Automated DNS Monitoring and Dangling Record Detection
mitigations.description: Deploy continuous monitoring solutions to identify and alert on dangling DNS records before attackers can exploit them. Use Microsoft Defender for App Service, AWS Route 53 health checks, or third-party tools like SubdomainSleuth to detect CNAME records pointing to deprovisioned resources.

code_example.code:
#!/bin/bash
# Azure: Use Microsoft's Get-DanglingDnsRecords PowerShell tool
# https://github.com/Azure/Azure-Network-Security/tree/master/Azure%20DDoS%20Protection/Dangling%20DNS

# Install Azure PowerShell module
pwsh -Command "Install-Module -Name Az -AllowClobber -Scope CurrentUser"

# Download Microsoft's dangling DNS detection script
wget https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Azure/Azure-Network-Security/master/Azure%20DDoS%20Protection/Dangling%20DNS/Get-DanglingDnsRecords.ps1

# Run detection across all Azure subscriptions
pwsh -File Get-DanglingDnsRecords.ps1 -SubscriptionId "YOUR_SUBSCRIPTION_ID"

# Alternative: Use SubdomainSleuth (Yahoo's open-source tool)
git clone https://github.com/yahoo/SubdomainSleuth
cd SubdomainSleuth
pip install -r requirements.txt

# Scan for dangling DNS records
python subdomainsleuth.py --domain example.com --provider aws,azure,github

# AWS Route 53: Create health check for CNAME targets
aws route53 create-health-check \
  --health-check-config \
    IPAddress=192.0.2.1,Port=443,Type=HTTPS,\
    ResourcePath=/health,FullyQualifiedDomainName=api.example.com,\
    RequestInterval=30,FailureThreshold=3

# Set up CloudWatch alarm for failed health checks
aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm \
  --alarm-name dangling-dns-alert \
  --alarm-description "Alert on potential dangling DNS" \
  --metric-name HealthCheckStatus \
  --namespace AWS/Route53 \
  --statistic Minimum \
  --period 300 \
  --evaluation-periods 2 \
  --threshold 1 \
  --comparison-operator LessThanThreshold \
  --alarm-actions arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:security-alerts

# Automated daily scan with cron
cat > /etc/cron.daily/dns-takeover-check << 'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
DATE=$(date +%Y-%m-%d)
LOGFILE="/var/log/subdomain-checks-${DATE}.log"

# Run multiple detection tools
subjack -w /tmp/subdomains.txt -t 100 -timeout 30 -o ${LOGFILE} -ssl
nuclei -l /tmp/subdomains.txt -t subdomain-takeover/ -severity high,critical >> ${LOGFILE}

# Alert if vulnerabilities found
if grep -q "VULNERABLE" ${LOGFILE}; then
    mail -s "URGENT: Subdomain Takeover Detected" security@example.com < ${LOGFILE}
fi
EOF

chmod +x /etc/cron.daily/dns-takeover-check

nist_csf: DE.CM-1 - The network is monitored to detect potential cybersecurity events
DE.CM-7 - Monitoring for unauthorized activity is performed
validation_steps: Run Get-DanglingDnsRecords.ps1 across all Azure subscriptions
Verify CloudWatch alarms trigger on failed health checks
Test SubdomainSleuth against known vulnerable subdomain
Confirm security team receives alerts for dangling DNS
Document response procedures for dangling DNS findings

mitigations.title: Implement Automated DNS Cleanup Workflow and Resource Deprovisioning Procedures
mitigations.description: Establish strict policies requiring DNS record removal before deprovisioning cloud resources. Implement Infrastructure-as-Code (IaC) with automated cleanup scripts that delete DNS records when associated resources are destroyed.

code_example.code:
# Terraform configuration with lifecycle management
# file: main.tf

resource "aws_s3_bucket" "app_assets" {
  bucket = "myapp-assets-${var.environment}"
  
  lifecycle {
    prevent_destroy = false
  }
}

resource "aws_route53_record" "app_assets_cname" {
  zone_id = aws_route53_zone.primary.zone_id
  name    = "assets.${var.domain_name}"
  type    = "CNAME"
  ttl     = 300
  records = [aws_s3_bucket.app_assets.bucket_regional_domain_name]
  
  # Ensure DNS record is deleted before S3 bucket
  depends_on = [aws_s3_bucket.app_assets]
}

# Pre-destroy hook to verify DNS cleanup
resource "null_resource" "dns_cleanup_check" {
  triggers = {
    bucket_id = aws_s3_bucket.app_assets.id
  }
  
  provisioner "local-exec" {
    when    = destroy
    command = "python verify_dns_cleanup.py ${aws_route53_record.app_assets_cname.fqdn}"
  }
}

# Python script: verify_dns_cleanup.py
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import sys
import dns.resolver
import time

def verify_dns_removed(fqdn, max_attempts=5):
    """
    Verify DNS record has been removed before proceeding with resource deletion.
    Prevents dangling DNS by ensuring cleanup order.
    """
    for attempt in range(max_attempts):
        try:
            answers = dns.resolver.resolve(fqdn, 'CNAME')
            if answers:
                print(f"Attempt {attempt + 1}: DNS record still exists, waiting...")
                time.sleep(10)
        except (dns.resolver.NXDOMAIN, dns.resolver.NoAnswer):
            print(f"✅ DNS record successfully removed: {fqdn}")
            return True
    
    print(f"❌ ERROR: DNS record still exists after {max_attempts} attempts")
    print(f"Manual intervention required for: {fqdn}")
    sys.exit(1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) != 2:
        print("Usage: verify_dns_cleanup.py <fqdn>")
        sys.exit(1)
    
    verify_dns_removed(sys.argv[1])

# GitHub Actions workflow for automated DNS cleanup verification
# file: .github/workflows/dns-cleanup.yml
---
name: DNS Cleanup Verification

on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - 'terraform/**'
      - 'infrastructure/**'

jobs:
  verify-dns-cleanup:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Terraform Plan
        id: plan
        run: |
          cd terraform
          terraform init
          terraform plan -destroy -out=destroy.tfplan
      
      - name: Extract DNS Records to be Removed
        run: |
          terraform show -json destroy.tfplan | \
          jq -r '.resource_changes[] | select(.type=="aws_route53_record" and .change.actions[0]=="delete") | .change.before.fqdn' \
          > dns_records_to_cleanup.txt
      
      - name: Verify No Dangling DNS
        run: |
          while IFS= read -r record; do
            echo "Checking if resource exists for: $record"
            # Check if CNAME target is still reachable
            target=$(dig +short "$record" CNAME)
            if [ ! -z "$target" ]; then
              # Verify target resource exists
              curl -I "https://$target" || echo "⚠️ Warning: Target may be unreachable"
            fi
          done < dns_records_to_cleanup.txt

# Service deprovisioning checklist script
# file: scripts/safe-deprovision.sh
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

RESOURCE_NAME="$1"
DOMAIN_NAME="$2"

echo "🔍 Pre-deprovisioning checks for: $RESOURCE_NAME"

# Step 1: Find all DNS records pointing to this resource
echo "[1/4] Finding DNS records..."
aws route53 list-resource-record-sets --hosted-zone-id "$HOSTED_ZONE_ID" | \
  jq -r ".ResourceRecordSets[] | select(.ResourceRecords[]?.Value | contains(\"$RESOURCE_NAME\")) | .Name"

# Step 2: Verify no active traffic
echo "[2/4] Checking CloudWatch metrics for active traffic..."
aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics \
  --namespace AWS/S3 \
  --metric-name AllRequests \
  --dimensions Name=BucketName,Value="$RESOURCE_NAME" \
  --start-time $(date -u -d '7 days ago' +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S) \
  --end-time $(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S) \
  --period 86400 \
  --statistics Sum

# Step 3: Remove DNS records first
echo "[3/4] Removing DNS records..."
aws route53 change-resource-record-sets \
  --hosted-zone-id "$HOSTED_ZONE_ID" \
  --change-batch file://dns-delete-changeset.json

# Wait for DNS propagation
echo "⏳ Waiting 5 minutes for DNS propagation..."
sleep 300

# Step 4: Now safe to delete resource
echo "[4/4] Deleting resource: $RESOURCE_NAME"
aws s3 rb "s3://$RESOURCE_NAME" --force

echo "✅ Resource safely deprovisioned without dangling DNS"

iso27001_controls: A.12.5.1 - Installation of software on operational systems
nist_csf: PR.IP-3 - Configuration change control processes are in place
PR.IP-2 - A System Development Life Cycle is implemented
validation_steps: Test Terraform destroy with dependent DNS records
Verify DNS cleanup script fails if record still exists
Confirm GitHub Actions workflow blocks PRs with dangling DNS risks

mitigations.title: Implement CAA DNS Records and Domain Verification Requirements
mitigations.description: Deploy DNS Certification Authority Authorization (CAA) records to restrict which Certificate Authorities can issue certificates for your domains. Implement domain verification requirements on cloud platforms to prevent unauthorized resource claiming.

code_example.code:
#!/bin/bash
# Configure CAA records to prevent unauthorized certificate issuance

# AWS Route 53: Add CAA records
cat > caa-record-changeset.json << 'EOF'
{
  "Changes": [
    {
      "Action": "UPSERT",
      "ResourceRecordSet": {
        "Name": "example.com",
        "Type": "CAA",
        "TTL": 300,
        "ResourceRecords": [
          {"Value": "0 issue \"letsencrypt.org\""},
          {"Value": "0 issuewild \"letsencrypt.org\""},
          {"Value": "0 iodef \"mailto:security@example.com\""}
        ]
      }
    }
  ]
}
EOF

aws route53 change-resource-record-sets \
  --hosted-zone-id Z1234567890ABC \
  --change-batch file://caa-record-changeset.json

# Verify CAA records
dig CAA example.com +short
# Expected output:
# 0 issue "letsencrypt.org"
# 0 issuewild "letsencrypt.org"
# 0 iodef "mailto:security@example.com"

# Azure: Enable custom domain verification for App Service
az webapp config hostname add \
  --webapp-name myapp \
  --resource-group myResourceGroup \
  --hostname subdomain.example.com

# Verify domain ownership with TXT record
az webapp show \
  --name myapp \
  --resource-group myResourceGroup \
  --query customDomainVerificationId \
  --output tsv

# Add verification TXT record to DNS
VERIFICATION_ID=$(az webapp show --name myapp --resource-group myResourceGroup --query customDomainVerificationId -o tsv)

cat > txt-verification.json << EOF
{
  "Changes": [{
    "Action": "UPSERT",
    "ResourceRecordSet": {
      "Name": "asuid.subdomain.example.com",
      "Type": "TXT",
      "TTL": 300,
      "ResourceRecords": [{"Value": "\"${VERIFICATION_ID}\""}]
    }
  }]
}
EOF

aws route53 change-resource-record-sets \
  --hosted-zone-id Z1234567890ABC \
  --change-batch file://txt-verification.json

# GitHub Pages: Enable domain verification
# 1. Add TXT record for _github-pages-challenge-{org}
# 2. Go to Organization Settings > Pages > Verified domains
# 3. Add domain and verify

cat > github-verification.json << 'EOF'
{
  "Changes": [{
    "Action": "UPSERT",
    "ResourceRecordSet": {
      "Name": "_github-pages-challenge-myorg.example.com",
      "Type": "TXT",
      "TTL": 300,
      "ResourceRecords": [{"Value": "\"a1b2c3d4e5f6\""}]
    }
  }]
}
EOF

# AWS S3: Implement bucket naming convention with random suffix
# This prevents attackers from guessing bucket names
RANDOM_SUFFIX=$(openssl rand -hex 8)
BUCKET_NAME="myapp-assets-${RANDOM_SUFFIX}"

aws s3 mb "s3://${BUCKET_NAME}"

# Store bucket name in Parameter Store for reference
aws ssm put-parameter \
  --name /myapp/s3-bucket-name \
  --value "${BUCKET_NAME}" \
  --type SecureString

# Update DNS to point to randomized bucket name
cat > s3-cname-update.json << EOF
{
  "Changes": [{
    "Action": "UPSERT",
    "ResourceRecordSet": {
      "Name": "assets.example.com",
      "Type": "CNAME",
      "TTL": 300,
      "ResourceRecords": [{"Value": "${BUCKET_NAME}.s3.amazonaws.com"}]
    }
  }]
}
EOF

aws route53 change-resource-record-sets \
  --hosted-zone-id Z1234567890ABC \
  --change-batch file://s3-cname-update.json

# Cloudflare: Enable DNSSEC
curl -X PATCH "https://api.cloudflare.com/client/v4/zones/{zone_id}/dnssec" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer ${CF_API_TOKEN}" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  --data '{"status":"active"}'

# Verify DNSSEC is active
dig +dnssec example.com | grep -i rrsig

validation_steps: Verify CAA records with: dig CAA example.com
Test unauthorized certificate issuance is blocked
Confirm Azure domain verification TXT record exists
Verify GitHub Pages domain verification is active
Test that S3 buckets use randomized names
Enable DNSSEC and verify with dig +dnssec

indicators: NXDOMAIN or SERVFAIL DNS responses for organization subdomains
SSL/TLS certificates issued for organization domains by unexpected CAs
HTTP 404 errors or 'NoSuchBucket' responses on critical subdomains
Unusual certificate transparency log entries for organization domains
Spike in DNS queries for rarely-used or deprecated subdomains
Phishing reports mentioning legitimate organization subdomains

log_queries.query:
// Detect potential dangling DNS via failed DNS resolutions
DnsEvents
| where TimeGenerated > ago(24h)
| where ResultCode in ("NXDOMAIN", "SERVFAIL")
| where Name endswith ".example.com"
| summarize FailedQueries = count() by Name, ResultCode, bin(TimeGenerated, 1h)
| where FailedQueries > 10
| order by FailedQueries desc

// Detect unauthorized SSL certificate issuance
AzureActivity
| where TimeGenerated > ago(7d)
| where OperationNameValue == "Microsoft.CertificateRegistration/certificateOrders/write"
| where ActivityStatusValue == "Success"
| where InitiatedBy !in ("authorized_admin@example.com")
| project TimeGenerated, Caller, ResourceId, Properties

// Monitor for deleted App Service resources with active DNS
AzureActivity
| where TimeGenerated > ago(24h)
| where OperationNameValue == "Microsoft.Web/sites/delete"
| join kind=inner (
    DnsEvents
    | where Name contains "azurewebsites.net"
) on $left.ResourceId == $right.Name
| project DeletedTime=TimeGenerated, ResourceName=ResourceId, StillQueried=Name

# Detect CNAME records pointing to non-existent resources
index=dns sourcetype=dns
| where query_type="CNAME"
| where response_code="NXDOMAIN" OR response_code="SERVFAIL"
| regex query=".*\\.(example\\.com|example\\.net)$"
| stats count by query, response_code, answer
| where count > 5
| sort -count

# Identify S3 NoSuchBucket errors on organization domains
index=web_logs sourcetype=access_combined
| where status=404
| where response_body LIKE "%NoSuchBucket%" OR response_body LIKE "%Code: NoSuchBucket%"
| where host LIKE "%.example.com"
| stats count by host, uri, remote_ip
| where count > 3

# Monitor certificate transparency logs for unexpected certificates
index=security sourcetype=certificate_transparency
| where common_name="*.example.com"
| where issuer NOT IN ("Let's Encrypt", "DigiCert", "Authorized CA Name")
| table _time, common_name, issuer, serial_number, not_before, not_after
| sort -_time

// Detect subdomain takeover via DNS monitoring
sequence by dns.question.name with maxspan=1h
  [dns where dns.response_code == "NXDOMAIN" and dns.question.name like "*.example.com"]
  [dns where dns.response_code == "NOERROR" and dns.question.name like "*.example.com"]

// GitHub Pages takeover detection
http where 
  url.domain like "*.example.com" and 
  http.response.status_code == 404 and 
  http.response.body like "*There isn't a GitHub Pages site here*"

// AWS S3 bucket takeover detection  
http where
  url.domain like "*.example.com" and
  http.response.body like "*NoSuchBucket*" and
  http.response.status_code == 404

tools.name: can-i-take-over-xyz
tools.usage: Reference guide listing 76+ services vulnerable to takeover with exploitation methods and fingerprints

compliance: SOC2 (CC6.1 - Logical and Physical Access Controls)
